retrieved_docs.title: Airway Suctioning(Archived) -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The use of normal saline while suctioning is not recommended by the American Association of Respiratory Care. The duration of suctioning should be less than 15 seconds per suction attempt. Following airway suction, the patient should be allowed to recover for at least 10 to 15 seconds and re-oxygenate as needed before re-suctioning occurs. Standard precautions should be followed while suctioning by the care provider. [1] Open vs. closed

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Airway Suctioning -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: This activity provides a comprehensive review of the indications, techniques, and safety protocols for surgical airway suctioning, with an emphasis on preoxygenation, sterile technique, appropriate equipment selection, and continuous patient monitoring. This activity also reinforces best practices in infection prevention and patient monitoring, enabling clinicians to deliver safer and more effective respiratory care for critically ill or chronically ventilated patients. Additionally, this activity underscores the importance of interprofessional collaboration among healthcare providers, enhancing their ability to refine airway management skills and minimize procedural complications. This activity also reinforces evidence-based practices, including the use of as-needed rather than routine suctioning, to support individualized, patient-centered care.

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Airway Suctioning -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: These indications guide the timely and appropriate use of suctioning to maintain airway patency, prevent complications, and support respiratory function.

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Airway Suctioning -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Suctioning is crucial for maintaining adequate ventilation in patients with compromised respiratory mechanics, particularly those who are unable to clear secretions independently. While there are no absolute contraindications to suctioning, the procedure carries inherent risks that require careful clinical judgment. Patients with preexisting trauma to the upper airway should undergo suctioning with caution, as further manipulation may worsen the injury.

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Airway Suctioning -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Individuals with temporary or long-term artificial airways (eg, endotracheal or tracheostomy tubes) where normal mucociliary clearance is impaired.

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Airway Suctioning -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Cases where airway secretions need to be collected for culture, laboratory analysis, or research purposes.

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Airway Suctioning -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with copious respiratory secretions who are unable to clear them independently, leading to respiratory distress or an increased risk of infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Airway Suctioning -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Objectives: Identify appropriate indications for surgical airway suctioning, including patients with artificial airways, neuromuscular disorders, and impaired secretion clearance. Screen patients for risk factors that may require suctioning, such as excessive secretions, altered consciousness, or neuromuscular conditions. Select the appropriate catheter size, suction pressure, and equipment for each patient based on their airway size, age, and condition to minimize risks and improve patient outcomes. Collaborate with interdisciplinary healthcare teams to develop comprehensive care plans for patients requiring frequent suctioning, ensuring safe and effective airway management. retrieved_docs.title: Airway Suctioning(Archived) -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: While there are no absolute contraindications, the healthcare provider must consider the clinical condition of the patient and possible adverse events that may occur with suctioning.

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Airway Suctioning -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: The catheter should never be advanced past the point of resistance or deep enough to cause trauma. [1] Specific guidelines recommend advancing the catheter 5 to 6 inches for nasopharyngeal suction and 3 to 4 inches for oropharyngeal suction, stopping earlier if a cough is elicited. [1] For tracheostomy suctioning, the procedure must be performed through a non-fenestrated inner cannula to prevent injury to the tracheal wall.

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Airway Suctioning -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Infants and children with respiratory conditions or ventilatory difficulties complicated by excessive secretions.

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Airway Suctioning -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Surgical airway suctioning is a vital procedure for clearing secretions from the upper and lower respiratory tracts in patients who cannot maintain airway patency independently. Indications include artificial airways, neuromuscular disorders, altered consciousness, excessive secretions, and aspiration risk. The procedure requires specialized equipment, including suction catheters, oxygen delivery systems, and monitoring devices, as well as strict adherence to sterile techniques and the use of personal protective equipment to minimize the risk of infection. Clinicians must skillfully select the appropriate catheter size, adjust suction pressure, and time the procedure appropriately while closely monitoring the patient's responses throughout the process. These steps help prevent complications such as mucosal trauma, hypoxia, and bradycardia. Effective suctioning is essential for preventing airway obstruction, enhancing oxygenation, and reducing infection and ventilator-related complications, particularly in patients with artificial airways or impaired secretion clearance due to sedation, neuromuscular diseases, or altered mental status.

retrieved_docs.title: Extubation -- Technique or Treatment
retrieved_docs.content: Frequent airway suction should be considered to prevent re-intubation.

retrieved_docs.title: Surgical Airway Suctioning -- Preparation
retrieved_docs.content: Proper preparation for airway suctioning is essential and includes selecting suction settings and equipment sizes appropriate to the clinical scenario. Suction pressure should be adjusted based on the patient’s age to ensure safety and effectiveness: Adults: 100 to 150 mm Hg Children: 100 to 120 mm Hg Infants: 80 to 100 mm Hg Newborns: 60 to 80 mm Hg

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 869
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation encompasses the specific procedure of تنبيب الرغامى, indicating that this intubation technique is a subcategory within the broader practice of managing the airway for ventilation. In addition, تنبيب الرغامى is also linked to trachea and bronchus operations, reflecting its relevance to surgical interventions involving the trachea and bronchi. Together, these relationships highlight that تنبيب الرغامى serves both as a component of respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation and as a procedure associated with tracheal and bronchial surgery, underscoring its importance in comprehensive airway management and operative care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 3 -- Community 2071
retrieved_docs.content: Insertion of a tracheal T‑tube is carried out by employing the technique of التنبيب (intubation), and the process of التنبيب itself encompasses the specific method of تركيب أنبوب فغر الرغامى (placement of a tracheal T‑tube). This relationship highlights that the procedural step of inserting a tracheal T‑tube relies on intubation as its underlying method, while intubation is defined in part by the act of installing the tracheal T‑tube. Understanding this linkage is important for clinicians to correctly apply the appropriate technique when managing airway interventions that require a tracheal T‑tube.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 23 -- Level 3 -- Community 1554
retrieved_docs.content: The CDISC SDTM Procedure Terminology includes the concept of respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation, which are essential airway management interventions used to secure the airway and provide assisted breathing in critically ill patients. It also incorporates the concept of thoracoscopic wedge resection of the lung, a minimally invasive surgical procedure performed to remove a small, wedge‑shaped portion of lung tissue for diagnostic or therapeutic purposes. By encompassing both of these procedures, the CDISC SDTM Procedure Terminology provides a standardized framework for capturing and reporting these distinct clinical interventions in clinical trial data, facilitating consistent documentation and analysis across studies.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 2539
retrieved_docs.content: Catheterization of the nasolacrimal duct, insertion of a permanent tube‑type catheter into the esophagus, and transglottic catheterization of the trachea are all procedures that employ the technique of التنبيب (intubation). In the nasolacrimal duct procedure, intubation is used to place a catheter within the tear‑drainage system to restore patency. The esophageal insertion represents a more specific application of intubation, involving the placement of a permanent tube to maintain long‑term access to the esophagus. Similarly, transglottic catheterization of the trachea utilizes intubation to introduce a catheter through the glottis into the airway for ventilation or therapeutic purposes. Together, these interventions illustrate how intubation serves as a common methodological foundation across diverse anatomical sites, guiding clinicians in the selection and execution of catheter‑based treatments.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1893
retrieved_docs.content: Implantation procedure is classified under the broader category of التنبيب (intubation). The Insertion of catheter into esophagus, permanent tube type (procedure) represents a more specific, narrower form of التنبيب, indicating a specialized application of intubation techniques. Transglottic catheterization of trachea employs التنبيب as its procedural method, illustrating that this airway access technique is performed through intubation. Together, these relationships highlight how various catheter‑based interventions are organized under the overarching concept of التنبيب, with some procedures serving as parent categories, others as specialized subtypes, and certain techniques directly utilizing intubation as their operative method.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1735
retrieved_docs.content: Trachea and bronchus operations represent a broad category of surgical and procedural interventions involving the airway, and within this category the specific procedure of تنبيب الرغامى (tracheal intubation) is included.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2636
retrieved_docs.content: Intubation (التنبيب) is a central procedural category that encompasses several related interventions and complications. The implantation procedure is classified under intubation as its parent process, indicating that implantation is performed within the broader context of airway management. Insertion of a tracheal T‑tube is described as a method that utilizes intubation, highlighting that successful placement of this specialized airway device depends on standard intubation techniques. Intubation is also linked to the potential adverse effect of corneal injury, reflecting that patients undergoing airway instrumentation may be at risk for ocular surface damage, especially when protective eye measures are not employed. Within the intubation domain, vascular edema (الوذمة الوعائية, SMQ) is listed as a member condition, suggesting that fluid accumulation in vascular tissues can be a clinical manifestation associated with or resulting from intubation procedures. Finally, the specific technique of placing a laryngeal tube (تركيب أنبوب فغر الرغامى) is identified as a method of intubation, illustrating another practical approach to securing the airway. Together, these relationships underscore the multifaceted nature of intubation, its procedural variants, associated risks, and related clinical entities, which are essential considerations for safe patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2148
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is classified as تركيب مسلك هوائي فموي بلعومي, indicating that this procedure involves the insertion of an oral and pharyngeal airway to establish and maintain a secure airway for assisted ventilation.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2535
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is defined as a category of procedures that includes the specific technique of تنبيب الرغامى (tracheal intubation). The procedure تنبيب الرغامى is also situated within the broader surgical domain of Trachea and bronchus operations, indicating that tracheal intubation is both a child of the Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation classification and a component of the larger group of Trachea and bronchus operations. This hierarchical organization clarifies how specific airway‑management techniques relate to broader operative categories in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 863
retrieved_docs.content: Airway management (zajištění dýchacích cest) is classified as a therapeutic procedure (إجراء علاجي) and is directly linked to respiratory therapy (المعالجة التنفسية). Therapeutic procedure is positioned under both respiratory therapy and intubation (التنبيب), reflecting its role in respiratory care and airway instrumentation. Intubation encompasses a range of airway‑related techniques, including placement of a laryngeal tube (تركيب أنبوب فغر الرغامى), transglottic tracheal catheterization, insertion of a permanent esophageal catheter, and insertion of a tracheal T‑tube, the latter being a form of tracheostomy (فغر القصبة الهوائية). Tracheostomy, itself a specific airway‑management procedure, serves as the parent category for broader medical procedures on the respiratory tract (Medical procedure on respiratory tract) and is contained within airway management. Both intubation and tracheostomy are associated with complications such as corneal injury and vascular edema (الوذمة الوعائية

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 25 -- Level 3 -- Community 2574
retrieved_docs.content: Obstetrical trachelotomy is presented as a component of routine obstetric care that includes antepartum care, vaginal delivery (with or without episiotomy and/or forceps), and postpartum care. In this context, obstetrical trachelotomy is regarded as synonymous with vaginal cesarean section, indicating that the procedure functions as a delivery method comparable to a cesarean performed through the vaginal route. Additionally, obstetrical trachelotomy is linked to the concept of a preventive incision, suggesting that the procedure may be employed prophylactically to reduce the risk of obstetric complications. Together, these relationships underscore the role of obstetrical trachelotomy within comprehensive maternal care, highlighting its integration with standard obstetric practices, its equivalence to a specific type of cesarean delivery, and its association with incision strategies aimed at preventing adverse outcomes.
